summary: This chunk discusses theoretical and contextual aspects of medieval necromancy—such as the perceived numinous power of magical books, the Book of Consecrations (Liber consecrationum), and the relationship between devotional and demonic practices—but does not contain specific spirit names, ritual ingredients, circle diagrams, incantation texts, or explicit references to Kieckhefer’s Table D or Munich Handbook CLM 849 beyond general description.
key_passages: A short, anonymous work called the Liber consecrationum {Book of Consecrations), which circulated in late medieval manuscripts in varying forms, makes clear that the book itself was a sacred object requiring elaborate consecration...
the operator must refrain from every pollution of mind and body, and for nine days must be abstinent in food and drink, must keep from idle or immoderate words, and must be clothed in clean garments.

by the power of these your most sacred names, On, Jesus Christ, Alpha and O, El, Eloy, Eloyye, Sithothith, Eon, Sepmelamaton, Ezelphates, Tetragramaton, Elyoram, Ryon, Deseryon, Erystion, Ysyornus, Onela, Baysyn, Moyn, Messias, Sother, Emanuel, Sabaoth, Adonay...

so that by the virtue, sanctity, and power of these names this book may be consecrated & and blessed ^ and confirmed ^... for consecrating the bond of spirits, and for consecrating all corrupt experiments...
